title: Plural Forms — Band 7+ Reference
subtitle: Every plural pattern an IELTS Band 7+ candidate is expected to control
intro: Band 7+ writing and speaking demands accurate plurals across regular, irregular, classical (Latin/Greek) and tricky compound forms — plus correct subject-verb agreement with collective and plural-only nouns. This is a complete reference: scan it, drill weak areas, and self-test against the checklist at the bottom.

sections.title: 1. Regular Plurals
sections.summary: The default rules. Most English plurals follow one of four spelling patterns based on the singular's ending.

rules.note: An extra syllable is pronounced (/ɪz/).

rules.pattern: Vowel + y → just add -s (no spelling change)
rules.note: Common error: writing *companys / *citys instead of companies / cities (after consonant) — or *daies / *keies (after vowel).

sections.title: 2. Words Ending in -f / -fe
sections.summary: These split into three groups. The third (both forms) is the trickiest.

rules.note: When both are valid, pick one and stay consistent within a piece of writing.

sections.tip: Memorise the -ves group as a list — there is no reliable rule that distinguishes 'leaf → leaves' from 'roof → roofs'.

sections.title: 3. Words Ending in -o
sections.summary: Three patterns — and choosing the wrong one is a common Band 6 spelling slip.

rules.pattern: Just add -s (often loanwords, abbreviations, music terms)
rules.note: Words ending in vowel + o (zoo, video, radio) almost always take just -s.

sections.title: 4. Irregular Plurals (Memorise)
sections.summary: These do not follow any spelling rule. You either know them or you don't.

rules.note: Compounds inherit the same change: policeman → policemen, footprint → footprints (only the 'foot' part stays — the other element follows its own rule).
Use 'persons' only in formal/legal contexts ('the persons named in the contract'). Everyday usage is always 'people'.

sections.title: 5. Zero Plurals (Singular = Plural Form)
sections.summary: These nouns have the same form for singular and plural; the verb tells you the number.

items: fish (plural 'fish' for the same species; 'fishes' for several species)

rules.note: Use the verb to mark number: 'One species is endangered; many species are extinct.'

examples.plural: two dozen eggs (NOT two dozens eggs)

rules.note: But with 'of', the -s comes back: 'dozens of eggs', 'hundreds of people', 'thousands of visitors'.

sections.title: 6. Latin & Greek Plurals (Academic Register)
sections.summary: The single highest-yield section for Band 7+ academic writing. Using 'criteria' (not *criterions) and 'analyses' (not *analysises) is exactly what examiners notice.

rules.note: Some accept Anglicised forms: 'cactuses', 'syllabuses', 'focuses' — fine in everyday writing, but classical forms read more academic.
Spelling looks identical to a verb form (axes = the plural of 'axis' AND of 'axe'). Context resolves it.
Common Band 6 error: 'criterias' / 'phenomenas' / 'a phenomena' — all wrong. 'Criteria' is already plural; for one, say 'a criterion'.
'Data' is now widely treated as singular ('the data shows...') in everyday English — both 'data is' and 'data are' are accepted. Academic writing prefers 'data are'.
'Indices' is preferred in maths/finance; 'indexes' in publishing (book indexes).
Anglicised '-mas' is more common in everyday writing; classical '-mata' appears in religious or technical texts.

sections.tip: Drop one classical plural per Task 2 essay where it fits naturally — 'these criteria', 'two hypotheses', 'underlying phenomena' — and Lexical Resource scoring rises noticeably.

sections.title: 7. Compound Nouns
sections.summary: Which word takes the -s? Two patterns, plus a tricky third.

rules.pattern: Pluralise the head noun (the most important word)
Pluralise the last word (most common compound nouns)
-ful suffix: pluralise the suffix, not the first noun
rules.note: Older form 'spoonsful' still appears but 'spoonfuls' is now standard.

sections.title: 8. Uncountable Nouns (Never Pluralise)
sections.summary: These cannot take -s and cannot be preceded by 'a / an / one'. They take a singular verb. To count them, use a partitive ('a piece of advice').

rules.pattern: Abstract nouns (most common Band 6 error zone)
items: news (already -s, but uncountable & singular: 'the news IS bad')
accommodation (NOT accommodations in BrE; AmE allows it)

examples.plural: a piece of advice / two pieces of advice
an item of news / three items of news

sections.tip: Saying 'I have many informations' or 'I gave him advices' is the fastest way to drop a band on accuracy. Train yourself into 'a lot of information' and 'some advice'.

sections.title: 9. Plural-only Nouns (Pluralia Tantum)
sections.summary: These nouns exist only in plural form and take a plural verb. To talk about one, use 'a pair of'.

rules.pattern: Items thought of as having two parts

examples.plural: a pair of trousers / two pairs of trousers
a pair of scissors / three pairs of scissors

rules.note: 'A pair of jeans IS expensive' (singular verb because 'a pair' is the subject); but 'These jeans ARE expensive' (plural verb because 'jeans' is the subject).

sections.title: 10. -s Ending But Singular Meaning
sections.summary: Some nouns end in -s but take a SINGULAR verb. Treating them as plural is a classic Band 6 error.

rules.note: 'Physics IS my favourite subject.' / 'Economics IS a difficult field.'
'Darts IS played on a wooden board.'

items: the United States ('The United States IS...')

rules.pattern: Same word, different meaning, different verb

sections.title: 11. Collective Nouns (BrE vs AmE)
sections.summary: Group nouns can take a singular or plural verb depending on whether you mean the entity or its members. The British and American conventions differ.

rules.pattern: British English (IELTS norm) — singular OR plural

examples.plural: The team ARE arguing among themselves.
The government HAS announced new policies.
The government ARE divided on the issue.

rules.note: BrE prefers plural when the action is something individuals do (argue, decide individually); singular when the entity acts as one.

examples.plural: The team IS playing well tonight.
The staff IS attending the meeting.

rules.pattern: Always plural (in both BrE and AmE)
items: cattle ('Cattle ARE grazing in the field.')
people (regular plural; 'persons' only in formal/legal use)

sections.tip: Pick one variety (BrE or AmE) and stay consistent throughout an essay. IELTS accepts both, but mixing within a paragraph reads as an accuracy slip.

sections.title: 12. Plurals That Change Meaning
sections.summary: Adding -s sometimes changes the meaning entirely. Confusing the two is a vocabulary error, not just a grammar one.

sections.title: 13. Subject-Verb Agreement Traps
sections.summary: Plurals interact with verbs through subject-verb agreement. These are the most-missed Band 7 patterns.

examples.plural: A number of students ARE absent. (= many students)
The number of students IS rising. (= the count itself)

rules.pattern: 'Each / every / one of + plural noun' → singular verb

examples.plural: Each of the students HAS submitted the work.
Every one of the books IS available.
One of my friends IS a doctor.

rules.note: Common error: 'Each of the students HAVE submitted...' — the subject is 'each', not 'students'.

examples.plural: Neither of them KNOWS the answer.

rules.pattern: 'Either... or / Neither... nor' → verb agrees with the NEAREST subject

examples.singular: either the students or the teacher
examples.plural: Either the students or the teacher IS responsible.

examples.singular: either the teacher or the students
examples.plural: Either the teacher or the students ARE responsible.

rules.pattern: 'The majority of / a lot of / some of / all of + plural' → plural verb

examples.plural: The majority of people ARE in favour.
A lot of children ARE absent today.

rules.note: But with uncountables, the verb is singular: 'The majority of the water IS contaminated.' / 'A lot of equipment IS missing.'

rules.pattern: 'There is / there are' — agrees with what FOLLOWS
rules.note: Common Band 6 slip: starting a sentence with 'There is' and then listing plural nouns ('There IS many reasons...' should be 'There ARE many reasons').

rules.pattern: Quantity expressions with apostrophe (treated as singular concept)

examples.plural: She has five years' experience in marketing.
Employees must give two weeks' notice.
It is an hour's walk from here.

rules.note: Hyphenated as adjective → singular: 'a 10-year-old child' (NOT 'a 10-years-old child'); 'a five-year plan' (NOT 'a five-years plan').

checklist: I can pluralise regular nouns including consonant + y, vowel + y, -f/-fe and -o variants without spelling errors.
I know the irregular plurals (men, women, children, people, mice, feet, teeth) and never write *childs / *foots / *womans.
I use classical plurals (criteria, phenomena, analyses, hypotheses, data) accurately in academic writing.
I count uncountables with partitives ('a piece of advice', 'an item of equipment').
I know that news, mathematics, physics, economics, politics (as a subject) take a SINGULAR verb despite ending in -s.
I use plural-only nouns (trousers, scissors, glasses) with plural verbs and 'a pair of'.
I can choose the right verb after 'a number of' (plural) vs 'the number of' (singular).
I use a singular verb after 'each of', 'every one of', 'one of', 'either of', 'neither of'.
I use a singular verb in hyphenated quantity adjectives ('a 10-year-old', 'a five-year plan').
